heroOverview: Hirsutism is the growth of coarse, dark, terminal hair in women and adolescent girls in a male-pattern distribution — face, chest, abdomen, and back — driven by excess androgen activity at the hair follicle. It affects roughly 5–10% of women of reproductive age worldwide and is the most common presenting sign of polycystic ovary syndrome, which accounts for approximately 70–80% of cases. The condition sits at the intersection of dermatology and endocrinology: the hair itself is a cosmetic and psychological concern, but the underlying hormonal excess carries metabolic risks including insulin resistance, dyslipidaemia, and subfertility. Most cases are manageable with a combination of hormonal suppression and physical hair removal, and identifying the cause guides both treatment and long-term health monitoring.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects approximately 5–10% of women of reproductive age globally (Endocrine Society, 2018)
keyStats.demographics: Almost exclusively affects women; typically presents in adolescence or early adulthood; prevalence varies by ethnicity — higher in South Asian and Mediterranean populations
keyStats.avgAge: Peak onset during adolescence and the third decade of life
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 70–80% of hirsutism cases are attributable to polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)

definition: Hirsutism (ICD-10: L68.0) is defined as the presence of terminal (coarse, pigmented) hair in androgen-sensitive areas of the female body where such hair is not normally found: specifically the upper lip, chin, sideburn area, chest, upper back, upper abdomen, and inner thighs. It is quantified using the modified Ferriman–Gallwey (mFG) scoring system, in which nine body areas are each scored 0–4; a total score ≥4–6 (threshold varies by ethnicity) is considered clinically significant. The underlying mechanism is androgen-stimulated conversion of vellus (fine, unpigmented) follicles into terminal follicles under the influence of dihydrotestosterone (DHT), the active androgen at the follicle level produced locally from testosterone by 5-alpha-reductase. Elevated circulating androgens, increased end-organ sensitivity, or both can produce the same phenotype. Hirsutism is classified as functional — arising from ovarian or adrenal androgen excess without a discrete tumour — or as rare cases secondary to androgen-secreting tumours or exogenous androgen intake. It falls under the purview of dermatology for hair assessment and treatment and endocrinology or gynaecology for hormonal evaluation and management. Hirsutism is distinct from hypertrichosis, which describes generalised excessive hair growth that is not androgen-driven and not limited to male-pattern distribution.

typesClassification.description: The most common cause, accounting for 70–80% of cases. Ovarian androgen excess drives elevated free testosterone, producing hair in the chin, upper lip, chest, and lower abdomen alongside oligomenorrhoea, acne, and metabolic features.

Hirsutism with a normal menstrual cycle and normal serum androgens, attributed to increased 5-alpha-reductase activity or heightened follicular sensitivity. Accounts for roughly 5–15% of cases and carries a benign metabolic prognosis.

A milder form of 21-hydroxylase deficiency presenting in adolescence or adulthood with androgen excess resembling PCOS; distinguished by an elevated 17-hydroxyprogesterone on ACTH stimulation testing.

Glucocorticoid excess, most often from a pituitary adenoma (Cushing disease), drives adrenal androgen overproduction; accompanied by central obesity, skin fragility, proximal myopathy, and hypertension.

Rare ovarian or adrenal tumours that secrete testosterone or DHEA-S at high levels. Onset is typically rapid (weeks to months), with virilisation (clitoromegaly, voice deepening). Total testosterone above 5 nmol/L or DHEA-S above 18.5 µmol/L warrants urgent imaging.
Androgenic progestogens (e.g. levonorgestrel), anabolic steroids, danazol, valproate, minoxidil, and ciclosporin can all cause or worsen hirsutism as a side effect.

primarySymptoms: Coarse, dark, terminal hair on the upper lip or chin in women, often the earliest noticed site and the one most associated with social distress.
Terminal hair on the chest, around the areolae, or in a midline sternal strip, areas where women typically have only fine vellus hair.
Hair growth on the upper back or shoulders, which patients often report discovering when changing clothing.
Midline lower abdominal hair extending downward from the umbilicus toward the pubic area.
Inner-thigh hair extending beyond the typical female pubic distribution into a diamond or escutcheon pattern.
Sideburn-area or preauricular facial hair, sometimes extending down the angle of the jaw.
Acne — co-occurring in up to 30% of women with PCOS-driven hirsutism because the same androgen excess stimulates sebaceous glands.
Menstrual irregularity or oligomenorrhoea, present in the majority of women with PCOS-associated hirsutism.
Scalp hair thinning or frontal hairline recession (androgenic alopecia), which can coexist in the same androgen-sensitive scalp follicles.
Psychological distress, reduced self-esteem, and social withdrawal, documented in surveys as more impairing than many chronic dermatological conditions.
earlyWarningSigns: Appearance of a few coarse hairs on the chin or upper lip in a teenage girl that were not present before puberty.
Gradual menstrual cycle lengthening beyond 35 days alongside new facial hair.
Persistent acne on the lower face and jawline that does not respond to standard topical treatment.
A rapidly increasing mFG score over weeks to months — rapid progression suggests a tumour rather than functional causes.
emergencySigns: Rapidly progressive hirsutism developing over weeks rather than months, suggesting an androgen-secreting tumour.
Clitoromegaly, voice deepening, or male-pattern baldness alongside hirsutism (virilisation) — these features mandate urgent evaluation.
Severe abdominal or pelvic pain alongside new hirsutism, which could indicate an ovarian tumour.
Signs of adrenal crisis in a patient being investigated for congenital adrenal hyperplasia — weakness, low blood pressure, hypoglycaemia.

causes.description: Dysfunctional ovarian theca cells overproduce testosterone and androstenedione; combined with reduced hepatic SHBG production (driven by hyperinsulinaemia), free testosterone rises and occupies follicular androgen receptors, converting vellus to terminal hair in androgen-sensitive areas. Accounts for 70–80% of hirsutism cases.

In up to 15% of hirsute women, circulating androgens and menstrual cycles are entirely normal. Increased local 5-alpha-reductase activity in the skin converts testosterone to DHT at a higher-than-normal rate, or the androgen receptor itself is more sensitive; the result is the same terminal follicle conversion without elevated serum androgens.

Partial deficiency of 21-hydroxylase (CYP21A2 gene) diverts adrenal steroid precursors toward androgen synthesis; 17-hydroxyprogesterone accumulates and is converted to androstenedione and testosterone. The milder non-classical form accounts for 1–6% of hirsute women and is more prevalent in Ashkenazi Jewish and Hispanic populations.

Cortisol excess — from pituitary, adrenal, or ectopic ACTH sources — drives adrenal androgen overproduction alongside elevated cortisol. The glucocorticoid also suppresses SHBG, raising free androgen fractions. Central obesity, striae, and proximal weakness accompany the hair changes.

Rare but important: Sertoli–Leydig cell tumours and adrenal adenomas or carcinomas secrete testosterone or DHEA-S at concentrations that rapidly virilise the patient. Total testosterone above 5 nmol/L or DHEA-S above 18.5 µmol/L prompts urgent cross-sectional imaging of adrenal glands and ovaries.

Anabolic steroids, androgenic progestogens (levonorgestrel, norethisterone), danazol for endometriosis, valproate, and topical minoxidil can all elevate androgen activity or trigger vellus-to-terminal conversion. A thorough medication history is essential before attributing hirsutism to an endogenous cause.

Severe hypothyroidism reduces SHBG production, raising free testosterone fractions; it may also cause hypertrichosis independently. Thyroid function should be checked in hirsute women with fatigue, weight gain, and cold intolerance.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of PCOS or hirsutism
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of women with PCOS have two- to threefold increased risk, reflecting a polygenic predisposition involving genes regulating androgen biosynthesis and insulin signalling.

riskFactors.factor: South Asian, Middle Eastern, or Mediterranean ancestry
riskFactors.description: Population studies consistently show higher mFG scores and higher rates of clinically significant hirsutism in these ethnic groups compared to East Asian or northern European women, attributed to differences in androgen receptor sensitivity and 5-alpha-reductase activity.

Hyperinsulinaemia suppresses hepatic SHBG production and directly stimulates ovarian theca cells to produce more testosterone. Weight loss of as little as 5–10% of body weight can reduce androgen levels and mFG scores in overweight women with PCOS.

PCOS is simultaneously the main risk factor and main cause; women diagnosed with PCOS have a lifetime prevalence of hirsutism of around 70–80%, and androgen levels correlate with the degree of hair growth.

Androgenic progestogens (particularly levonorgestrel and norethisterone-containing contraceptives), anabolic steroids, danazol, and valproate all increase effective androgen activity. Switching to a non-androgenic or anti-androgenic regimen can markedly reduce progression.
Adrenal androgen secretion (DHEA-S) begins rising around age 6–8 (adrenarche) and peaks in the early 20s. Exaggerated adrenarche is associated with PCOS and early-onset hirsutism.
Sustained hypercortisolaemia can mildly elevate adrenal androgen output and impair SHBG synthesis, contributing to borderline androgen excess; stress management may have a modest beneficial effect.

affectedDemographics: Women of reproductive age (15–45 years), the primary affected group
Adolescent girls presenting with acne, irregular periods, and early facial hair
Overweight and obese women with associated insulin resistance
Women of South Asian, Middle Eastern, or Mediterranean descent, who show higher prevalence rates
Postmenopausal women, in whom declining oestrogen unmasks relative androgen excess
Women with a family history of PCOS, adrenal hyperplasia, or hirsutism
diagnosisOverview: Evaluation of hirsutism begins with characterising the hair distribution using the modified Ferriman–Gallwey score and reviewing the timeline of onset. Gradual onset over years with irregular menses strongly suggests PCOS; rapid onset over weeks raises concern for a secreting tumour. Baseline blood tests include total testosterone (or free testosterone if total is borderline), DHEA-S (adrenal androgen marker), 17-hydroxyprogesterone (to screen for non-classical congenital adrenal hyperplasia), thyroid-stimulating hormone, prolactin, and a morning fasting glucose and insulin for insulin resistance. Most guidelines recommend an early-morning 17-OHP measurement in the follicular phase; if above 6 nmol/L, a Synacthen stimulation test confirms NCCAH. PCOS is diagnosed by the Rotterdam criteria (≥2 of: oligo-anovulation, biochemical or clinical hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovarian morphology on ultrasound) after exclusion of mimicking disorders. Pelvic and adrenal ultrasound is performed initially; MRI of the adrenal glands and ovaries is reserved for suspected tumours or when total testosterone exceeds 5 nmol/L or DHEA-S exceeds 18.5 µmol/L. A 24-hour urinary free cortisol or low-dose dexamethasone suppression test is added when the clinical picture suggests Cushing syndrome.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Primary androgen screen; levels above the upper reference limit confirm biochemical hyperandrogenism. Very high total testosterone (>5 nmol/L) triggers imaging for an androgen-secreting tumour.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A morning blood draw, ideally in the early follicular phase; results available within 24–48 hours. Some labs report only total testosterone, so free testosterone is calculated from SHBG.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Adrenal androgen marker; markedly elevated levels (>18.5 µmol/L in premenopausal women) suggest an adrenal source — either NCCAH or a secreting tumour.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same blood draw as testosterone; no special timing required as DHEA-S fluctuates less across the day than cortisol or testosterone.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screening test for non-classical congenital adrenal hyperplasia (21-hydroxylase deficiency); elevated in the early morning follicular phase.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw before 09:00 in the first 1–5 days of the menstrual cycle. A basal level above 6 nmol/L prompts a Synacthen stimulation test to confirm the diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Hypothyroidism and hyperprolactinaemia are both reversible causes of hirsutism and menstrual irregularity; screening prevents missed diagnoses.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Included in the same blood panel; no special preparation needed beyond avoiding dopamine-affecting medications before prolactin measurement.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies insulin resistance, which drives androgen excess in PCOS and determines whether metformin or lifestyle intervention should be prioritised.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Overnight fast of 8–10 hours before the blood draw; results used to calculate HOMA-IR (glucose × insulin ÷ 22.5).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses ovarian morphology (polycystic appearance: ≥20 follicles per ovary or ovarian volume >10 mL on modern equipment) to support or refute PCOS diagnosis; also detects ovarian tumours.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A transvaginal scan offers better resolution in adults; transabdominal scanning is used in adolescents or those who are not sexually active. Takes 10–20 minutes and is painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Standardised clinical scoring of hair density in nine body regions (0–4 per site) to quantify hirsutism severity at baseline and monitor treatment response over time.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed during the physical examination by the clinician; takes 2–3 minutes and provides an objective severity score.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of hirsutism is guided by the underlying cause, severity, desire for contraception, and reproductive plans. For mild cases (mFG 4–8) with regular cycles and normal androgens, cosmetic approaches — laser hair removal, electrolysis, or eflornithine cream — may be all that is needed. For PCOS-associated or idiopathic hirsutism, first-line medical treatment is a combined oral contraceptive pill (COCP) containing an anti-androgenic progestogen (e.g. cyproterone acetate, drospirenone, or dienogest); the oestrogen component raises SHBG, reducing free testosterone, while the progestogen competitively blocks androgen receptors. Clinical benefit develops over 6–12 months because the hair follicle cycle is long. When the COCP alone is insufficient, spironolactone (an aldosterone antagonist with strong androgen-receptor blocking activity) is added at doses of 50–200 mg daily; it is the most evidence-supported anti-androgen in most Western countries. Finasteride (a 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor) and flutamide are alternatives in non-reproductive-aged women or with reliable contraception, given teratogenic risk. NCCAH is treated with low-dose glucocorticoid suppression of adrenal androgens, sometimes combined with the COCP. Androgen-secreting tumours require surgical removal. Weight loss of 5–10% in women with PCOS and insulin resistance can meaningfully reduce androgen levels. Cosmetic hair removal is used alongside medical treatment because medical therapy slows new growth but does not remove existing terminal hair.

medicalTreatments.name: Combined oral contraceptive pill (COCP)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line pharmacological treatment. The oestrogen component raises SHBG, lowering free testosterone; the progestogen competitively blocks androgen receptors in hair follicles. Anti-androgenic pills containing cyproterone acetate, drospirenone, or dienogest are preferred over those with androgenic progestogens (levonorgestrel, norethisterone).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces mFG score by approximately 40–60% after 12 months of use in randomised trials; clinical response visible from 6 months.

medicalTreatments.name: Spironolactone
medicalTreatments.description: An aldosterone antagonist that blocks androgen receptors and mildly inhibits androgen synthesis; used at 50–200 mg daily, usually combined with the COCP for contraception (it is teratogenic). The most widely used anti-androgen for hirsutism in countries without licensed anti-androgens.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces mFG score by approximately 40–50% over 6–12 months; effective in approximately 70–80% of users at adequate doses.

medicalTreatments.name: Finasteride
medicalTreatments.description: A 5-alpha-reductase type 2 inhibitor that reduces DHT production in hair follicles; used at 2.5–5 mg daily. Must be used with reliable contraception because of teratogenicity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces mFG score by roughly 30–50% after 12 months; similar or slightly less effective than spironolactone in head-to-head comparisons.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical eflornithine cream (13.9%)
medicalTreatments.description: Inhibits ornithine decarboxylase, an enzyme essential for follicle hair growth, when applied twice daily to facial skin. Does not remove hair but slows regrowth; approved for facial hirsutism and often used as an adjunct to laser treatments.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces facial hair growth rate in approximately 50–60% of treated women versus placebo in controlled trials; effect begins within 4–8 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Metformin
medicalTreatments.description: An insulin sensitiser that reduces ovarian androgen production indirectly by lowering hyperinsulinaemia in women with PCOS and insulin resistance. Less effective for hirsutism than the COCP but improves metabolic parameters and menstrual regularity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Produces modest mFG score reductions of approximately 10–20%; primarily used when metabolic benefits or fertility are the treatment goal.

alternativeTreatments.name: Laser hair removal
alternativeTreatments.description: Alexandrite, Nd:YAG, or diode laser selectively destroys melanin-containing follicles; most effective on dark hair against light skin. Multiple sessions (6–10) are required for a sustained 70–90% reduction in hair density. Recommended alongside medical therapy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Electrolysis
alternativeTreatments.description: An electric current destroys individual hair follicles; the only FDA-cleared method for permanent hair removal. Slower than laser and best suited for pale or fine hair that does not respond to laser. Requires many sessions.

alternativeTreatments.name: Intensive pulsed light (IPL)
alternativeTreatments.description: Broad-spectrum light targets follicle pigment; less specific than laser but widely available. Effective for facial hair, typically requiring 6–12 sessions, with approximately 50–60% long-term reduction.

alternativeTreatments.name: Weight loss and lifestyle modification
alternativeTreatments.description: In overweight women with PCOS, a 5–10% reduction in body weight reduces insulin levels, restores menstrual regularity, lowers free testosterone, and produces modest mFG score improvement — without medication side effects.

whySeeSpecialist: A dermatologist performs the initial mFG assessment and co-manages cosmetic hair removal options, while an endocrinologist or gynaecologist with PCOS expertise guides hormonal investigation and treatment. Specialist referral is warranted when androgens are markedly elevated, the onset is rapid, there is virilisation, or when first-line contraceptive pills have not produced adequate improvement after 12 months. Women planning a pregnancy need specialist guidance on which medications are safe to continue.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a dermatologist or endocrinologist with a specific interest in PCOS or androgen disorders. Many gynaecological-endocrinology clinics see hirsutism as a core referral. Check whether the clinician can order and interpret full androgen profiles, perform pelvic ultrasound, and offer both medical and laser/electrolysis co-ordination within the same service. If NCCAH or a tumour is suspected, a specialist endocrinologist at a hospital with adrenal imaging access is essential.
preventionStrategies: Maintain a healthy body weight and avoid insulin resistance through regular physical activity and a low-glycaemic diet, which reduces PCOS-driven androgen excess.
Choose hormonal contraceptives with an anti-androgenic or androgen-neutral progestogen (drospirenone, dienogest, cyproterone acetate) rather than androgenic ones if you are at risk.
Avoid androgenic medications such as anabolic steroids, danazol, and high-dose androgenic progestogens unless there is no alternative.
Screen family members of women with PCOS — sisters have a two- to threefold higher risk and can benefit from early lifestyle intervention.
Adolescent girls with irregular periods and acne should be evaluated early so that PCOS and associated metabolic risk are identified before hirsutism progresses.
lifestyleModifications: Aim for a BMI in the healthy range through dietary changes and regular aerobic and resistance exercise — even a 5% weight loss significantly lowers free testosterone in women with PCOS.
Follow a low-glycaemic-index diet rich in vegetables, legumes, and whole grains to reduce insulin peaks that drive androgen production.
Limit added sugars and refined carbohydrates, which exacerbate the hyperinsulinaemia central to PCOS-related hirsutism.
Use SPF 30+ sun protection if undergoing laser hair removal, since treatment is delayed by tan skin.
Track menstrual cycle length and symptoms to help clinicians monitor treatment response over the necessary 6–12 months.
Join a PCOS support group or seek psychological support if distress is significant — the psychosocial impact of hirsutism is well documented and undertreated.

recommended: Vegetables, legumes, and wholegrains to maintain a low glycaemic load
Lean protein at each meal to support satiety and reduce insulin spikes
Omega-3-rich foods (oily fish, walnuts, flaxseed) for anti-inflammatory benefit in PCOS
Dairy in moderation — evidence on dairy and androgen levels in PCOS is mixed; some women benefit from reducing intake
avoid: Refined carbohydrates and added sugars, which drive insulin surges and secondary androgen excess
Trans fats and ultra-processed foods, which worsen insulin resistance
Excess alcohol, which impairs hepatic SHBG production and raises free testosterone
Large portions of very high-glycaemic foods (white bread, sugary drinks, pastries) as regular staples

exerciseGuidelines: At least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic exercise per week is recommended, consistent with general cardiovascular and metabolic guidelines (WHO, 2020). Resistance training two to three times per week improves insulin sensitivity independently of weight loss and is particularly beneficial in PCOS-related hirsutism. Exercise need not be intense to be effective: brisk walking, cycling, or swimming are all appropriate. Avoid exercising immediately after a laser or IPL session if the treated skin is still red or inflamed.
dailyManagement: Apply eflornithine cream twice daily to the face if prescribed, consistently — gaps of more than a few days allow regrowth to resume at the pre-treatment rate.
Take the contraceptive pill or spironolactone at the same time each day to maintain steady hormone levels.
Shave or use depilatory cream for immediate cosmetic control between laser sessions; shaving does not worsen hirsutism, despite the common belief.
Record mFG scores every 3–6 months with photographs to objectively track treatment progress.
Contact the prescribing clinician promptly if new virilisation symptoms — voice change, clitoromegaly — develop, as these require urgent reassessment.
prognosis: The long-term prognosis of hirsutism is generally good when the cause is identified and treated. PCOS-associated and idiopathic hirsutism respond to sustained hormonal therapy, with the majority of patients achieving a meaningful reduction in mFG score (40–60%) over 6–12 months. Laser hair removal combined with medical therapy produces the best cosmetic outcomes. Discontinuing anti-androgen therapy typically leads to regrowth within 6–12 months, so most women continue treatment until they decide to pursue pregnancy or reach natural menopause, when the relative androgen excess often moderates. NCCAH-associated hirsutism responds well to low-dose glucocorticoids and may not require lifelong treatment. Women with PCOS require long-term metabolic monitoring regardless of hirsutism control, given the associated risks of type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease. Androgen-secreting tumours, once resected, rarely recur if completely excised.
recoveryTimeline: Cosmetic improvement from medical treatment takes 6–9 months because the anagen hair cycle dictates how quickly existing terminal follicles regress; patients should expect to see meaningful improvement by 6–12 months and peak response at 12–24 months of consistent therapy. Laser hair removal produces noticeable thinning after 3–4 sessions (spaced 4–8 weeks apart) and a 70–90% reduction in hair density after a full course of 6–10 sessions; maintenance sessions every 6–12 months may be needed. Eflornithine cream shows effect within 4–8 weeks of consistent use.
complications: Psychological impact — anxiety, depression, body dysmorphia, and social withdrawal are well-documented complications that may require dedicated psychological support.

Metabolic complications of PCOS — untreated or undertreated PCOS-related hirsutism is associated with long-term insulin resistance, type 2 diabetes (5- to 10-fold increased risk), dyslipidaemia, and cardiovascular disease.
Subfertility and anovulatory infertility — the androgen excess driving hirsutism in PCOS also disrupts ovulation; timely treatment and specialist management improve fertility outcomes.
Drug side effects — spironolactone can cause hyperkalaemia, menstrual irregularity, and dizziness; finasteride and flutamide are teratogenic and contraindicated in pregnancy.
Delayed diagnosis — virilising tumours are rare but rapid; failure to recognise the clinical signs of a secreting tumour delays surgery and allows progression.

supportResources.name: Verity — PCOS charity (UK)
supportResources.description: UK patient organisation for women with PCOS and hirsutism offering peer support, information, and clinician directories.

supportResources.name: PCOS Awareness Association
supportResources.description: International organisation providing education and support resources for women with polycystic ovary syndrome and related conditions including hirsutism.

supportResources.name: Endocrine Society — Patient Resources on Hirsutism
supportResources.description: Plain-English patient education on the hormonal causes, evaluation, and treatment of hirsutism from the world's leading endocrinology society.

sources.title: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Evaluation and Treatment of Hirsutism in Premenopausal Women (2018)
Rotterdam consensus on diagnostic criteria and long-term health risks related to polycystic ovary syndrome. Human Reproduction, 2004
Martin KA et al. Evaluation and treatment of hirsutism in premenopausal women. Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism, 2008
NICE CG171 — Fertility: assessment and treatment for people with fertility problems (2013, updated 2017)
Azziz R et al. The prevalence and features of the polycystic ovary syndrome in an unselected population. Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism, 2004
Teede H et al. International evidence-based guideline for the assessment and management of polycystic ovary syndrome (2018)
Ferriman D, Gallwey JD. Clinical assessment of body hair growth in women. Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism, 1961

confusedWithConditions.name: Polycystic ovary syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PCOS is the most common cause of hirsutism, not a mimic — but women are sometimes told they have 'just' hirsutism when PCOS is the underlying diagnosis; the distinction matters for metabolic and fertility management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Androgenic alopecia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Androgenic alopecia causes scalp hair loss at the same androgen-sensitive follicles; it is a co-occurring sign, not a separate condition, but is sometimes mistakenly attributed to unrelated causes rather than hyperandrogenism.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acne vulgaris
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acne on the lower face and jawline driven by androgen excess shares the same hormonal root as hirsutism in PCOS, but is sometimes treated in isolation without investigating or addressing the underlying androgen excess.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypothyroidism can cause hypertrichosis (diffuse non-androgenic hair growth) and reduced SHBG; it is distinguished by a markedly elevated TSH and is fully reversible with levothyroxine replacement.

relatedConditions.name: Polycystic ovarian syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: The primary underlying cause of hirsutism in approximately 75% of cases, sharing the same androgen-excess mechanism and requiring co-management.

relatedConditions.name: Cushing disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Pituitary ACTH excess drives adrenal androgen overproduction alongside cortisol, producing hirsutism with central obesity, striae, and proximal weakness.

relatedConditions.name: Hyperprolactinaemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Elevated prolactin suppresses gonadotropins and can cause menstrual irregularity and mild androgen excess that mimics PCOS-associated hirsutism.

relatedConditions.name: Secondary amenorrhoea
relatedConditions.relevance: Persistent anovulation from androgen excess causes secondary amenorrhoea, which commonly co-presents with hirsutism in women with PCOS.

relatedConditions.name: Endometriosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Danazol treatment for endometriosis is an androgenic drug that can cause or worsen hirsutism as a medication side effect.

faqs.question: What is hirsutism?
faqs.answer: Hirsutism is the growth of coarse, dark hair in women in areas where such hair is normally only found in men, such as the face, chest, and abdomen. It is caused by excess androgen hormones acting on hair follicles and affects roughly 5–10% of women of reproductive age.

faqs.question: What causes hirsutism in women?
faqs.answer: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is the cause in around 75% of cases. Other causes include idiopathic hyperandrogenism, non-classical congenital adrenal hyperplasia, Cushing syndrome, androgen-secreting tumours, and certain medications including anabolic steroids and androgenic contraceptive pills.

faqs.question: Is hirsutism the same as PCOS?
faqs.answer: No, but PCOS is the most common cause of hirsutism. Hirsutism refers specifically to the hair-growth symptom, while PCOS is a broader hormonal condition that also involves ovarian cysts, irregular periods, and metabolic features. Hirsutism can also occur without PCOS.

faqs.question: How is hirsutism diagnosed?
faqs.answer: A doctor scores hair distribution using the modified Ferriman–Gallwey scale and checks blood testosterone, DHEA-S, 17-hydroxyprogesterone, TSH, prolactin, and fasting insulin. A pelvic ultrasound assesses ovarian morphology. Rapid-onset or severe hirsutism triggers adrenal and ovarian imaging.

faqs.question: Can hirsutism be cured?
faqs.answer: Hirsutism from functional causes such as PCOS is managed rather than cured — hormonal treatment and hair removal significantly reduce hair growth, but stopping treatment usually leads to regrowth within 6–12 months. Hirsutism from a removable cause, such as a tumour or a drug, can resolve completely.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for facial hirsutism?
faqs.answer: Combining anti-androgen medication (a pill with anti-androgenic progestogen or spironolactone) with laser hair removal produces the best results. Eflornithine cream slows regrowth on the face and is used between laser sessions. Improvement takes 6–12 months of consistent treatment.

faqs.question: Does the contraceptive pill help hirsutism?
faqs.answer: Yes. Combined oral contraceptive pills raise SHBG, which lowers free testosterone, and the progestogen blocks androgen receptors in hair follicles. Pills containing anti-androgenic progestogens such as cyproterone acetate or drospirenone are preferred. Most women see improvement after 6–12 months.

faqs.question: Does spironolactone work for hirsutism?
faqs.answer: Spironolactone at 50–200 mg daily blocks androgen receptors and reduces hair growth in approximately 70–80% of women. It is usually combined with the contraceptive pill, both for added efficacy and because it can cause birth defects if pregnancy occurs during treatment.

faqs.question: Will losing weight help hirsutism?
faqs.answer: In overweight women with PCOS, a 5–10% body weight reduction lowers insulin levels, reduces ovarian testosterone production, and produces measurable improvement in the Ferriman–Gallwey score. Weight loss does not help women with normal weight or non-PCOS causes.

faqs.question: Does laser hair removal work for hirsutism?
faqs.answer: Laser hair removal is highly effective for cosmetic control, reducing hair density by 70–90% after 6–10 sessions. It targets the melanin in dark, coarse hair, so it works best on dark hair against light skin. Medical treatment to reduce androgen levels first improves laser outcomes.

faqs.question: How long does treatment for hirsutism take to work?
faqs.answer: Medical treatments such as the contraceptive pill or spironolactone take 6–9 months to show visible improvement because the hair growth cycle is slow; peak response occurs at 12–24 months. Laser hair removal shows thinning after 3–4 sessions spaced 4–8 weeks apart.

faqs.question: Is hirsutism a sign of cancer?
faqs.answer: Very rarely. Rapidly progressive hirsutism developing over weeks — especially with virilisation such as voice deepening or clitoromegaly — can indicate an androgen-secreting ovarian or adrenal tumour. Gradual hirsutism over years in a woman with irregular periods is almost always due to PCOS or a benign hormonal imbalance.

faqs.question: Can hirsutism go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Idiopathic hirsutism (normal cycles, normal androgens) is stable and does not worsen significantly over time but does not resolve without treatment. PCOS-driven hirsutism tends to persist. After menopause, declining oestrogen relative to androgens can temporarily worsen the condition before it stabilises.

faqs.question: What is the difference between hirsutism and hypertrichosis?
faqs.answer: Hirsutism is androgen-driven hair growth in a male-pattern distribution in women. Hypertrichosis is diffuse, non-androgen-dependent excess hair growth that can affect anyone and any body area. The distinction matters because the causes and treatments differ completely.

faqs.question: Can men get hirsutism?
faqs.answer: No — hirsutism is defined in women. Men with androgen-sensitive follicles grow terminal hair throughout the body as part of normal male physiology. Male hair disorders include hypertrichosis and androgenic alopecia, but not hirsutism by clinical definition.

faqs.question: What blood tests check for hirsutism?
faqs.answer: The standard panel includes total testosterone (or free testosterone), DHEA-S, 17-hydroxyprogesterone, TSH, prolactin, and fasting glucose with insulin. An elevated testosterone above 5 nmol/L or DHEA-S above 18.5 µmol/L requires adrenal and ovarian imaging.

faqs.question: Does hirsutism affect fertility?
faqs.answer: Hirsutism itself does not impair fertility, but its most common underlying cause — PCOS — causes anovulation that results in subfertility in many women. Treating the hormonal imbalance often restores regular ovulation. Fertility specialists can guide ovulation induction when natural conception is not occurring.

faqs.question: Can teenagers get hirsutism?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hirsutism often first appears in adolescence alongside irregular periods and acne, and PCOS frequently presents at or shortly after puberty. Adolescent girls with these features should be evaluated by a gynaecologist or endocrinologist so that metabolic risks are identified and managed early.

faqs.question: Is eflornithine cream effective for hirsutism?
faqs.answer: Eflornithine 13.9% cream applied twice daily to the face slows regrowth in approximately 50–60% of women compared with placebo, but does not remove existing hair. It works best as an adjunct to laser treatment or anti-androgen therapy, reducing regrowth between sessions.

faqs.question: Should I shave if I have hirsutism?
faqs.answer: Shaving is safe and does not make hirsutism worse — it does not increase hair count, coarseness, or growth rate, despite the widespread belief. Shaving is a practical cosmetic option between medical treatments or laser sessions and carries no clinical downside.

metaDescription: Hirsutism causes coarse, dark hair growth on the face and body in women due to androgen excess. Learn about PCOS-linked causes, diagnosis, and treatment.
